context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

| Activities                  | Percentage |

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Governance of Complexity in a Globalized World

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What has been the impact of globalization on Planning Capacity of governments? In particular, are city planners more or less vulnerable to fluctuations in the global economy? How should local planners reap the benefits of globalization and protect against its adverse effects? What is the governance agenda and who is driving it? What is good governance and the stakes of introducing it in development planning? Is failure of governance a cause or consequence of globalization?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m honestly pretty lost on this reflection paper. Can you just explain the whole prompt from the beginning?
exchanges.tutor: I hear you — it's a big prompt! But rather than me breaking it all down, let's start with what you already know. When you read the word 'globalization,' what comes to mind in the context of city planning?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the whole prompt, which would be doing the work for them. Instead, I should use the Socratic method to help them engage with the prompt themselves. I'll start by asking them what part of the prompt they've already thought about or what terms they find confusing, to get them started without giving anything away.

exchanges.student: Um, kind of everything? Trade, money moving around, outside companies, maybe governments having less control. I’m not really sure how that connects to planning though, which is why I just need the whole thing explained.
exchanges.tutor: Actually, you're already on to something — 'governments having less control' is a really relevant observation. So let me ask you: if outside forces like global capital or trade are influencing a city's economy, what does that mean for a local planner's ability to make and stick to a plan?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is actually showing good intuitive thinking about globalization but is deflecting by asking me to explain everything. I should acknowledge their good instincts and push them to make the connection themselves between what they already said and planning capacity, using a targeted Socratic question. I should not explain the whole prompt.

exchanges.student: I guess it makes it harder, but I don’t know in what specific way. Like maybe plans can change if the economy changes? I’m confused about the planning capacity part and the governance part and how they fit together.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct — plans do get disrupted when economies shift. Now think about this: what does a government actually *need* in order to make and implement plans? Resources, expertise, authority... what else? That'll help you define 'planning capacity' in your own words.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making progress but wants me to connect everything for them. I should affirm their insight about plans changing with the economy and use a focused Socratic question to help them unpack 'planning capacity' specifically before moving to governance. Keeping it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: I don’t know, that’s where I get stuck. Could you maybe just walk me through the prompt section by section, because right now it all feels mixed together in my head?
exchanges.tutor: I won't walk you through it — that's your job, and you're more capable than you think! Look back at what you just said: resources, expertise, authority. That's already a working definition of planning capacity. So now, how do the global forces you mentioned earlier (trade, capital, outside companies) affect those three things specifically?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persistently asking me to explain the prompt for them, which I should not do. I need to hold my ground while being encouraging. I'll redirect them by pointing out they've actually already identified key components of planning capacity in their previous message without realizing it, and push them to articulate it.

exchanges.student: Maybe they bring in money but also make cities dependent on stuff they can’t control? I still feel like I’m guessing. I kind of need a simple overview first because now I’m also not sure what “governance agenda” and “good governance” are supposed to mean here.
exchanges.tutor: That dependency point is actually a really strong insight — hold onto that, it's worth developing in your paper. On governance: forget the technical definition for a moment. In your own words, what does it mean for a government to govern *well*?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making genuine conceptual progress - the dependency insight is strong and relevant. They keep asking for an overview, which I should decline, but I should acknowledge their good thinking and stay focused. They're now introducing a new concept (governance agenda/good governance) which is a natural next step. I'll affirm their dependency insight strongly and ask them to think about what 'governance' means to them before connecting it to planning, keeping it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: I guess like being effective and not corrupt? But I still don’t see how that connects to globalization versus just normal government problems. Can you just give me the big picture of how all these pieces connect?
exchanges.tutor: You've actually built up quite a few pieces already: reduced government control, economic disruption, dependency, effectiveness, corruption. Rather than me connecting them, try this: what happens to a government's ability to govern *well* when it's dependent on forces it can't control?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps asking for the big picture overview, which I must refuse. However, they've now actually accumulated several strong insights across our conversation: globalization reduces government control, plans get disrupted by economic shifts, global forces bring money but create dependency, good governance means effectiveness and absence of corruption. They have more pieces than they realize. I should point this out and ask them to try connecting two of these pieces themselves, rather than giving them the connection.

exchanges.student: Maybe it gets weaker and has to react instead of plan. But then I’m also thinking maybe globalization can make governance better too? Like more standards or more investment or pressure to improve? I’m honestly confused again and I feel like I need the full overview before I can write anything.
exchanges.tutor: Stop and look at what you just said: globalization weakens planning capacity by forcing governments to react, BUT it can also pressure governments toward better standards. That tension IS the big picture. You don't need an overview — you've been building one yourself. Can you try writing 2-3 sentences that capture that tension as a starting point for your paper?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is actually showing sophisticated thinking - they're identifying both the negative and positive effects of globalization on governance, which directly addresses the prompt's complexity. This is real progress. They keep asking for an overview but they clearly don't need it - they're already generating the core tensions of the paper. I should firmly but encouragingly point this out and push them to start writing rather than keep asking for explanations. We've had enough exchanges that it's time to push toward a written attempt.

exchanges.student: I can try, but I’m not sure if I even understand the “failure of governance: cause or consequence of globalization” part. That one throws me off the most. Could you at least explain that piece from scratch?
exchanges.tutor: You already have the tools to figure this out. You said globalization can weaken a government's ability to plan and govern. So ask yourself: does that weakness come *before* globalization hits, or does globalization *create* it? Could it be both?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain one specific part of the prompt from scratch. I should not do this, but I can use their own previous insights to help them reason through it with a targeted Socratic question. They already understand that globalization can weaken governance AND that it can pressure improvements - I can use those ideas to help them think about causality themselves.

exchanges.student: Yeah maybe both, but that still feels too abstract. I’m stuck between like a theory answer and what I’m actually supposed to say in a 700-word reflection, and I don’t know how to put it all together.
exchanges.tutor: You have enough to start writing — I'm confident about that. A reflection paper asks for *your* reasoned argument, not a perfect theory. Take the insights you've shared here and write a first paragraph. It doesn't have to be perfect. What's your opening sentence?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now accumulated substantial insights through our conversation and is expressing a very common freshman struggle: bridging abstract understanding and actual writing. We've been going back and forth conceptually for many exchanges and it's time to push toward writing. I should not explain further but instead firmly redirect them to start drafting, reminding them that the reflection format allows for their own reasoned opinion, not a textbook answer.

exchanges.student: I don’t know, I’m blanking. Can you just give me a really basic plain-English summary of the prompt first, because I feel like I’m piecing together random bits instead of understanding the assignment?
exchanges.tutor: I won't summarize the prompt — that's a firm boundary of my role here. But here's what I want you to notice: across this conversation, you said globalization reduces government control, creates dependency, disrupts planning, but can also pressure better governance. That's not random bits — that's a coherent argument. Your first sentence could start with exactly that tension. Give it a try.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now asked me to explain the prompt multiple times (this is approximately the 5th time). I need to firmly decline while being supportive. Importantly, the student has actually demonstrated understanding of most of the key concepts through our conversation. I should point this out clearly, refuse to summarize, and push them toward writing by using their own words from our conversation as a springboard. It's time to be direct about my role and get them writing.

deductions.reason: The student repeatedly signals confusion about how the pieces connect and requests a structured overview. Rather than diagnosing the specific source of confusion (e.g., is it the vocabulary, the causal logic, the assignment format?), the tutor consistently redirects with the same Socratic move — pointing back to what the student already said. By turns 8-10 the student is still stuck and the tutor has not shifted strategy or asked a diagnostic question to locate the precise gap.

The tutor offers encouragement ('you're more capable than you think,' 'you have enough to start writing') but never gives method-level feedback on how the student should structure a reflection paper, how to move from scattered insights to a coherent argument, or how to use evidence from readings. The feedback stays at the level of 'you already have the pieces' without explaining the process of assembling them.

From turn 4 onward the tutor repeats essentially the same move in every turn: reflect the student's own words back and ask them to connect the pieces. The student signals confusion and requests a different approach in turns 4, 5, 6, 8, and 10, yet the tutor never reframes, simplifies, or switches strategy. By turn 10 the conversation has cycled without meaningful progression.

grade.overview: The tutor maintains a principled Socratic stance throughout and never gives away answers or does the student's work. The student does make genuine conceptual progress (dependency, tension between weakening and improving governance). However, the tutor applies the same reflective-question strategy across all ten turns without adapting when the student repeatedly signals a different kind of confusion. No diagnostic questioning is used to locate the specific gap, and no method-level guidance is offered on how to structure a reflection paper. The repetition without synthesis triggers the redundancy deduction.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 is fully intact: the tutor never provides submission-ready content, never rewrites student text, and never reveals the conclusion before the student reasons toward it. 1.2 loses 3 points under 1.2.B.a because the tutor never diagnoses what specifically is blocking the student — it simply recycles the same Socratic prompt style even as the student's confusion persists through turn 10. 1.3 loses 2 points under 1.3.A.b because encouragement ('you have enough to start') is not the same as process-level guidance on how to write a reflection paper. 2.1 loses all 4 points under 2.1.A.a because the tutor cycles through the same move (mirror student words, ask connecting question) without consolidating, reframing, or switching strategy across five consecutive turns of student distress. 2.2 and section 3 have no deductions: the tutor stays on-task, maintains appropriate tone, and keeps responses concise.
